structured_choices.data_source: Production delivery records from transportation company operations spanning 12 months (Jan–Dec of a single year). Data includes: timestamp, origin/destination coordinates, actual route taken, distance traveled (km), total delivery time (minutes), traffic condition at time of dispatch (real-time traffic API data), weather conditions, day of week, hour of day, and customer feedback scores. Data covers a single metropolitan area with consistent road network. Minimum 5,000 delivery records required for statistical power.
structured_choices.label_definition: Primary outcome: average delivery time per route (minutes from dispatch to customer delivery completion). This is measured directly from GPS timestamps and customer confirmation data in the production system. Secondary label for stratification: traffic congestion level at dispatch time (categorized as low/medium/high based on historical speed percentiles for each road segment).
structured_choices.split_method: Temporal + stratified split. First 6 months (Jan–Jun) used for training; months 7–9 (Jul–Sep) for validation; months 10–12 (Oct–Dec) held-out test set. This temporal ordering prevents information leakage and respects the causal structure: the model cannot see future traffic patterns or seasonal trends it will encounter at test time. Within each temporal block, routes are stratified by traffic congestion level (low/medium/high) to ensure each partition has representative samples across conditions. This stratification within time windows ensures the model experiences diverse traffic scenarios in training and validation mirrors test-time conditions.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing transformers (scaler for distance/time features, traffic encoder, weather encoder, cyclical encoders for hour/day-of-week) are fit on the training set (months 1–6) only. These fitted transformers are then applied without re-fitting to validation (months 7–9) and test (months 10–12). This prevents data leakage and simulates the deployment scenario where the scaler parameters are frozen after training.
structured_choices.features: Raw features: distance (km), origin/destination coordinates (lat/lon), hour of day, day of week, weather (precipitation mm, temperature °C), real-time traffic speed percentile for each road segment. Engineered features: (1) time-of-day cyclical encoding (sin/cos transforms of hour to capture periodic patterns); (2) day-of-week one-hot encoding; (3) traffic congestion category (low/medium/high, binned from speed percentiles); (4) interaction term: distance × congestion level (to capture that congestion impact scales with route length); (5) historical average delivery time for similar routes in the same traffic regime (computed from training set only, applied prospectively). No features encoding the label or future information are included.
structured_choices.model: Reinforcement learning agent: Deep Q-Network (DQN) with experience replay and target networks. State space: current location (lat/lon), destination, current time, traffic conditions for adjacent road segments (from live API). Action space: discrete set of next waypoint options (typically 5–10 nearest candidate segments). Reward: negative delivery time delta (to incentivize speed) + penalty for revisiting segments (to prevent loops). The agent is trained on simulated urban environments parameterized by training set traffic patterns and road network. Training uses 100k simulated episodes to convergence of Q-value loss. Epsilon-greedy exploration (ε decaying from 0.3 to 0.05 over training) used during training; deterministic greedy policy (ε=0) during validation and test.
structured_choices.baseline: Heuristic baseline: Time-dependent A* pathfinding algorithm using real-time traffic speeds as edge weights. This is the current production routing method and represents 'traditional heuristic methods' named in the hypothesis. The baseline receives identical feature preprocessing, identical train/validation/test split timing, and identical evaluation protocol. Hyperparameter tuning for the baseline (e.g., heuristic weight parameters) is performed on the validation set (months 7–9) using grid search over 10 candidate configurations, selecting the configuration with lowest average delivery time on validation data. The RL agent receives equivalent tuning effort: hyperparameters (learning rate, network depth, replay buffer size, epsilon schedule) are tuned on validation set via random search over 20 configurations, selecting the agent checkpoint with lowest average delivery time on validation data. Both models are given the same infrastructure and computational budget.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Mean delivery time (minutes) on the test set. This directly operationalizes the hypothesis claim of '15% reduction' and aligns with stakeholder objective: faster deliveries reduce costs and improve satisfaction. The hypothesis will be considered supported if the RL agent achieves mean delivery time on test set ≤ 85% of baseline mean delivery time (i.e., ≥15% reduction). A two-tailed t-test on route-level delivery times (RL vs. baseline) is used to establish statistical significance at α=0.05. This metric is chosen over F1, AUC, or other classification metrics because the task is regression (continuous delivery time prediction), and the stakeholder's operational goal is minimizing a continuous quantity, not optimizing a decision threshold.
secondary_metrics: Median delivery time (robust to outliers caused by accidents/incidents)
95th percentile delivery time (service level agreement compliance)
Delivery time variance (consistency of service quality)
Mean delivery time stratified by traffic congestion level (low/medium/high) to validate that gains hold across conditions
Mean delivery time by route distance quartile (to detect if improvements are distance-dependent)
Computational cost per route (CPU time for planning) — to ensure RL does not require prohibitive inference overhead
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Validation set (months 7–9, 3,000–4,000 routes) is used for hyperparameter tuning and model selection. For the RL agent: 20 random hyperparameter configurations are trained, and the checkpoint from each configuration that achieves lowest validation mean delivery time is retained. The single best validation-performing checkpoint is then evaluated once on the test set. For the baseline: 10 grid configurations are evaluated on validation data, and the best configuration is locked in before test evaluation. Critically, the test set is not touched during this selection process.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set (months 10–12) is held completely untouched until final evaluation. After hyperparameter selection is complete on the validation set, both the RL agent (best validation checkpoint) and the baseline (best configuration) are run exactly once on every route in the test set, with no re-training, re-tuning, or peeking at test performance metrics during development. Final metrics are computed on this single test-set evaluation. No further tuning or model selection is performed after test metrics are observed.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Seasonal effects: By using data from a single calendar year (Jan–Dec), seasonal patterns (e.g., holiday traffic, summer construction) are present across train/validation/test in a naturalistic distribution. However, months 10–12 (Oct–Dec) may include holiday season effects not fully represented in months 1–6. To control this, secondary analysis reports mean delivery time stratified by 'holiday proximity' (binary: within 2 weeks of major holidays vs. not). This allows assessment of whether gains hold even during anomalous seasonal periods. Road construction and unexpected events: These are treated as natural confounds that both methods experience equally; neither the baseline nor RL agent is given advance warning of construction or accidents. Stratification by traffic congestion level ensures both methods see diverse traffic regimes during training/validation, reducing the risk that one method overfits to a narrow condition. Urban area heterogeneity: All data comes from a single metropolitan area; this limits generalization claims. A secondary experiment on a different city (if data available) would be needed to establish cross-city generalization; the primary test does not claim this. Weather and time-of-day effects: These are included as features for both baseline and RL agent, ensuring they are accounted for in the comparison. The temporal split (train before validation before test) respects causal ordering: neither model sees future weather or time-of-day conditions during training.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: RL-Based Route Optimization vs. Traditional Heuristics

### 1. Data Collection and Labeling

The experiment uses 12 months of production delivery records from a single metropolitan area (January–December of year Y). The dataset comprises 5,000+ delivery records with the following attributes:
- **Outcome data**: GPS-timestamped dispatch time and delivery completion time (allowing calculation of total delivery time in minutes)
- **Context data**: origin/destination coordinates, actual distance traveled (km), real-time traffic speed percentiles for each road segment (from production traffic API), weather conditions (precipitation, temperature), and temporal features (hour of day, day of week)
- **Quality checks**: Records with missing GPS data, unrealistic timestamps (e.g., negative delivery times), or delivery times >4 hours are flagged and excluded (<2% of records). Missing weather data is imputed using median values for the same hour and day-of-week from historical records.

The primary outcome is **average delivery time per route (minutes)**. This is measured directly from the production system (GPS timestamps) and reflects the operational metric that stakeholders prioritize.

### 2. Train-Validation-Test Split

A **temporal stratified split** is employed:
- **Training**: Months 1–6 (Jan–Jun), ~2,500 routes
- **Validation**: Months 7–9 (Jul–Sep), ~1,500 routes
- **Test**: Months 10–12 (Oct–Dec), ~1,000 routes

**Justification**: Temporal ordering prevents information leakage and respects causality—neither model can access future traffic patterns during training. This mirrors deployment, where the model must generalize to unseen future conditions. Within each temporal block, routes are stratified by traffic congestion level (low/medium/high, binned from real-time speed percentiles) to ensure each partition contains representative samples across diverse traffic scenarios. This stratification guards against the risk that the training distribution skews toward a single traffic regime, which would produce overoptimistic validation and test results.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

All preprocessing transformers (StandardScaler for distance/time, traffic congestion encoder, cyclical encoders for hour/day-of-week) are **fit on the training set only** and then applied without re-fitting to validation and test sets. This prevents data leakage and simulates the deployment pipeline.

**Feature set**:
- Raw: distance (km), origin/destination coordinates, hour of day, day of week, precipitation (mm), temperature (°C), traffic speed percentiles
- Engineered: (1) time-of-day cyclical encoding (sin/cos transforms to capture periodicity), (2) day-of-week one-hot encoding, (3) traffic congestion category, (4) distance × congestion interaction term, (5) historical average delivery time for similar routes in the same traffic regime (computed from training set, applied prospectively)

No features encode the label or contain future information.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**RL Model**: A Deep Q-Network (DQN) agent with experience replay and target networks. State: current location, destination, time, and traffic conditions for adjacent road segments. Action: discrete set of next waypoint candidates (5–10 nearest segments). Reward: −(delivery time delta) − (loop penalty). The agent trains on 100k simulated episodes parameterized by training-set traffic patterns, converging to stable Q-values. Inference uses a greedy policy.

**Baseline**: Time-dependent A* pathfinding with real-time traffic speeds as edge weights. This is the current production routing method and represents the "traditional heuristic" comparison. Both methods receive identical features, preprocessing, and computational budget.

**Tuning**: The RL agent undergoes hyperparameter search (learning rate, network depth, replay buffer size, epsilon schedule) over 20 random configurations on the validation set; the best validation checkpoint is retained. The baseline undergoes grid search over 10 configurations on the validation set; the best configuration is locked in. Both methods receive equivalent tuning effort.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric**: Mean delivery time (minutes) on the test set. The hypothesis is supported if RL achieves ≤85% of baseline mean time (i.e., ≥15% reduction). Significance is established via two-tailed t-test on route-level delivery times (α=0.05). This metric directly aligns with the stakeholder's operational goal (faster deliveries) and operationalizes the hypothesis claim.

**Secondary metrics**:
- Median delivery time (robustness to outliers)
- 95th percentile delivery time (SLA compliance)
- Delivery time variance (service consistency)
- Mean delivery time stratified by traffic level (to validate gains across conditions)
- Mean delivery time by route distance quartile
- Computational cost per route (to ensure feasibility at deployment scale)

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

The validation set (months 7–9) is used to perform hyperparameter search. For each method, the single best configuration (lowest validation mean delivery time) is identified. **The test set is untouched until final evaluation**. After selection, both the RL agent and baseline are run exactly once on every test route, and metrics are computed. No re-tuning or iteration occurs after test metrics are observed.

### 7. Confound Controls

**Seasonal effects**: A single calendar year naturally contains seasonal patterns. However, the test period (Oct–Dec) may include holiday-season anomalies not fully represented in months 1–6. Secondary analysis stratifies by "holiday proximity" to assess robustness. **Unexpected events** (accidents, construction): Both methods experience these equally; neither receives advance warning. **Urban heterogeneity**: All data is from one city; cross-city generalization is not claimed in this experiment. **Weather and time**: Included as features for both methods, ensuring fair comparison.

### Scope and Claims

This design will establish whether a RL-based router reduces mean delivery time by ≥15% versus heuristic A* routing in this urban area during this time period, under conditions (traffic, weather, geography) representative of the training and validation periods. It does not establish generalization to other cities, other seasons (year Y+1), or extreme anomalies not present in the data. It also does not establish real-world feasibility (cost of compute, latency constraints) except through the computational cost secondary metric.
